summary: The metal bust of Vladimir Lenin that my grandmother Janaki brought back from the USSR in 1984 has always fascinated me. It stands about 10 inches tall and shows Lenin in a graceful pose, giving a lecture. This bust is more than just a statue; it represents the Communist ideology that my grandparents embraced long before I was born. Having it in my possession connects me deeply to their intellectual and political legacy.

My grandmother Janaki traveled to the Soviet Union multiple times in the 1970s and 80s to learn Russian and became fluent. She brought back the Lenin bust from the USSR in 1984, which has become a cherished family heirloom. The Soviet Union was the place where she deepened her connection to the Russian language and Communist ideology, which influenced our family’s worldview. It’s a reminder of the cultural and political ties my grandparents had with that part of the world.

India is where my paternal grandparents lived and where my grandmother Janaki became a renowned Russian teacher. In the 1970s and 80s, she taught Russian to thousands of students, including those from the former Soviet Union who came to India to learn traditional music, dance, and languages. The Lenin bust’s unique smell takes me back to India and the years I spent there, reminding me of my grandparents and their cultural world. India was the backdrop for much of their intellectual and political life.

My grandmother Janaki decided to learn the Russian language in her forties and became fluent after multiple trips to the USSR in the 1970s and 80s. She became a highly sought-after Russian teacher in India, instructing thousands of students, including many from the Soviet Union. Her mastery of Russian was a key part of her identity and career, linking our family to the Soviet world. This language skill also helped bridge cultural and political connections that influenced our family’s history.

Communism was a significant part of my grandparents’ beliefs and shaped much of their worldview. My paternal grandfather Krishnan, a doctor and rationalist, found Communism appealing during the 1930s and 40s when he came of age. My grandmother also embraced Communist ideology, though I’m not sure if it was from her father or after marriage. Even though I don’t identify as a Communist, the ideology influenced my family’s intellectual discussions and my own interest in politics.

I am the first person in my family born in the United States to doctor-parents who emigrated in the 1970s. I moved to the U.S. for college in the late 1990s and later worked in Georgia starting in 2008. The Lenin bust, a symbol of my grandparents’ Communist beliefs, now holds special meaning for me here in the U.S., especially as a professor of American Politics. It’s ironic and somewhat subversive to have this symbol of an ideology often hated in the U.S. in my possession.

I started working in Georgia in 2008, and I had the Lenin bust with me in my office there. Georgia became a place where I could reflect on my family’s history and the political legacy symbolized by the bust. Living and working in Georgia, I found new meaning in owning an object that represents Communist ideology, especially given the political climate in the U.S. It’s a reminder of my grandparents’ intellectual influence and my own career in American politics.
